prediction.summary: A critical account takeover incident involving user sarah.chen has been detected. The incident began with an MFA bypass and an impossible travel alert, indicating a successful login from a new device in Singapore shortly after a login from London. The attacker subsequently created email forwarding rules to an external address and to delete security alerts, followed by VPN and SSH connections from the suspicious Singapore IP. The SIEM confirmed these activities as critical indicators of account compromise.

timeline.event: Successful authentication for user sarah.chen from London (203.0.113.88).
MFA bypass requested for user sarah.chen due to push fatigue from 203.0.113.88.
Successful authentication for user sarah.chen from Singapore (198.18.50.22) on a new device.
IMPOSSIBLE_TRAVEL alert triggered for user sarah.chen (London to Singapore in 2 minutes).
User sarah.chen created an inbox rule to forward all emails to attacker@dropmail.cc.
User sarah.chen created an inbox rule to delete security alerts.
User sarah.chen connected to VPN from 198.18.50.22 (first seen).
SSH publickey accepted for user sarah.chen from 198.18.50.22.
SIEM detected account takeover indicators: impossible travel + MFA anomaly + mail forwarding.
CRITICAL SIEM alert for user sarah.chen, technique T1078 Valid Accounts compromise.
Automated response forced admin password reset for sarah.chen.
IDS reported session hijack risk score 92 for user sarah.chen from 198.18.50.22.

recommendations: Immediately verify the identity and legitimacy of sarah.chen's recent activities and contact the user out-of-band.
Investigate the MFA bypass mechanism and its root cause to prevent future occurrences and ensure MFA integrity.
Perform a comprehensive forensic analysis of the compromised account and associated systems for further malicious activity, lateral movement, or data exfiltration.
Block the malicious IP address (198.18.50.22) at the perimeter and review all connections originating from it.
Review and remove any unauthorized inbox rules created by the compromised account and restore default settings.
